title: Water environment worker
change: Occupational standard and assessment plan revised
overviewOfRole: This role protects water environments, manages flood risks, prevents pollution, and ensures public safety while working with teams, contractors.

knowledges.detail: How to operate different assets and how they contribute to the protection of communities and the environment.
Maintenance practices, processes and procedures covering operational assets and plant.
How to enact plans if assets do not operate as expected or they exceed their operational tolerance.

Resources, materials, and techniques that support a sustainable approach to work delivery, including options for habitat creation, soft engineering methods for water flow control or retention, and approaches that minimise environmental impact while enhancing biodiversity and ecosystem resilience.
Techniques and materials for building or maintaining boundaries, barriers and signage relating to public safety or access.
Legal requirements and best practice for rights of way and public access routes, including the extent of legal powers where public access needs to be restricted.
Principles of vegetation management, including control of plant growth to ensure safety, reliability of assets, and environmental compliance.
How to communicate with internal and external stakeholders including which communication channels to use, active listening, empathy, how to manage challenging conversations and when to escalate.

Importance of business relationships with partners, for example other organisations, contractors, volunteers and communities, to ensure efficient and effective work and enhance the organisation’s local and national reputation.

The principles underpinning the Joint Emergency Services Interoperability Programme (JESIP), how to work with other category 1 responders and responsibilities that come under the Civil Contingencies Act (CCA).
How to plan and select plant, equipment and materials suitable for an activity, considering the site, sensitivity to local heritage, weather conditions and wear and tear.

Relevant health, safety and wellbeing legislation, organisational responsibilities, standards and risk management procedures including lone working systems, manual handling practices and procedures for working at height.

Hazards and risks associated with working in an unfamiliar and emergency working environment, for example in severe weather or near hazardous substances, and how to carry out dynamic risk assessments.
How to identify confined spaces, be aware of their associated risks and when to request specialist support.
The principles of how underground and overhead services are located and their associated risks.
The stages of an asset’s lifecycle and the organisation’s asset management system.
How operational decisions are made and informed by information such as on-line telemetry, water levels, flow and rainfall gauges, national and local weather forecasts.
What causes flooding and increased water levels in different water environments, and how to manage associated risks to on-site personnel.
Agricultural practices, including the causes and consequences of runoff, sedimentation, and watercourse erosion, along with their impact on ecosystems.

The nature and behaviour of different water environments and assets, both natural and engineered, and an awareness of different hazards within these environments, for example, seasonal change, tidal variations, changing weather conditions and water levels.

Environmental standards, legislation, practices and control measures in reference to biosecurity, waste management, cross contamination, impact on protected species and spread of Invasive Non Native Species (INNS) to preserve our natural environment.
Principles, legislation and sources of advice relating to the management of valued heritage, Sites of Special Scientific Interest (SSSIs) and historic structures.
Natural flood management techniques, their benefits and the impact they have on watercourses, including on water quality and biodiversity.
Relevant legislation that relates to bird nesting, protected species and how it affects permissions for flood risk and land drainage works.

behaviours.detail: Acts as an ambassador of the organisation’s values and behaviours.
Has a strong work ethic, takes responsibility for delivering work to a high standard and conducts themselves in a professional manner.
Willingness to suggest new ideas, offer feedback and seek guidance and support.
Willing to work as part of a team and independently in a way that achieves shared goals and supports an inclusive workplace.
Takes ownership of work, reflects on own performance and is willing to engage with their personal and professional development.

Has a positive attitude towards health and safety, proactively manages their personal wellbeing and actively follows guidance and procedures relevant to their training and responsibilities to maintain a safe working environment for themselves and others, and contribute to a culture of safety awareness.

skills.detail: Operate assets safely and effectively in accordance with environmental and organisational standards and procedures.
Use a range of techniques to maintain assets safely and effectively in accordance with environmental and organisational standards and procedures.
Enact plans when assets do not operate as expected, taking steps to minimise the impact on communities and customers.
Use sustainable materials and methods where possible to support biodiversity, reduce flood risk or improve ecosystem resilience.
Maintain or build safety signage and barriers or boundaries to protect the public.
Manage vegetation safely and effectively using a range of appropriate plant and machinery.
Communicate through the appropriate channels with the support of technology and digital tools.

Communicate effectively with internal and external stakeholders for example colleagues, partner organisations, local communities, volunteers and the general public, offering guidance and handling queries or concerns professionally.
Collect and record relevant information using appropriate technology and digital tools to ensure assets can be well maintained.
Select and operate appropriate plant and equipment, choosing suitable resources to carry out the activity safely and efficiently.
Work in line with health and safety legislation.
Identify hazards, carry out continuous dynamic risk assessments, implement control measures and challenge unsafe actions to protect yourself and others.
Work safely in or around water.
Apply manual handling techniques to safely handle and move materials and equipment.
Support asset management by working to local maintenance priorities, helping assess asset condition, and shaping work programmes that meet defined standards.
Use a range of practices and control measures to ensure compliance with legislation and standards relating to biosecurity, waste management and protected species.
Carry out work in-line with seasonal restrictions and check for nesting birds and protected species when carrying out vegetation management.

involvedEmployers: Environment Agency, Canal and River Trust, Wey and Arun Canal Trust, Upper Medway Internal Drainage Board.
otherInvolvedStakeholders: University Centre Somerset College Group, Riverlution, Lantra.
englishAndMathsQualifications: <p>English and maths qualifications must be completed in line with the <a rel="noopener" href="https://www.gov.uk/guidance/apprenticeship-funding-rules" target="_blank">apprenticeship funding rules</a>.</p>
reviewDetails: this apprenticeship will be reviewed in accordance with our change request policy.
clusterDescription: Manage and promote diverse environments, engaging with a range of stakeholders
banners: This revised version of the occupational standard and assessment plan is approved by Skills England under the 2025/2026 apprenticeship assessment reforms. It is available for information only pending a decision on the funding.

qualifications.title: award or training and assessment in emergency first aid at work +F
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation: Award or training and assessment in emergency first aid at work +F minimum 1 day course (face-to-face), carried out by either an awarding organisation, a UKAS accredited trade body or voluntary aid society recognised by government as specified in HSE document GEIS 3. Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is a regulatory requirement or is an essential component of any mandatory qualification

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in brushcutters and trimmers
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation: Lantra Awards Technical Award in Brushcutters/Trimmers – (training with assessment) or City & Guilds NPTC Level 2 Award in the Safe Use of Brush-cutters and Trimmers (601/1299/2). Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is a regulatory requirement or is an essential component of any mandatory qualification

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in winching
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation: Lantra introduction to winching in a land-based setting or Lantra introduction to winching in aquaculture or NPORS Winching & Recovery (N609). Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is a regulatory requirement or is an essential component of any mandatory qualification

professionalRecognition.level: This standard meets the requirements of Registered Environmental Technician – REnvTech.  Upon receipt of the apprenticeship certificate, individuals are eligible to apply for REnvTech through a shortened application route.  Individuals also need to be a member of a professional body that is licensed by the Society for the Environment to be awarded this status. Further information is on the SocEnv website.

duties.dutyDetail: Maintain a variety of water environment assets safely using appropriate mobile plant, tools or machinery.
Operate assets safely using appropriate equipment to maintain water level, flow or navigation.
Attend to events that impact upon the environment, waterways, homes, businesses, assets or natural habitats.
Work alongside internal teams and other external parties to deliver planned work.
Maintain, enhance, protect, restore or create habitats to ensure a natural healthy environment.
Support the planning and delivery of work activities as directed by their organisation and remit.
Identify the safety hazards and risks associated with working in or near a water environment and take appropriate action to mitigate any risks.
Identify the location and types of asset and structures within their operational area and adapt to account for environmental variations.
Prepare, maintain or construct boundaries, barriers and signage relating to access, to support the safe use of water-related environments.
Understand and apply sustainable practices to contribute to low carbon, biodiversity-rich and equitable solutions for people and environment.
Respond to assets by deploying plans when they exceed their operational tolerance or do not operate as expected.
Deliver work as part of an organisational response to flood or other environmental incidents, collaborating with other agencies and organisations where necessary.
Deliver work with consideration for how it may affect the wider operational area, environment and community.
